summary: O'Neil reviews 'The Wellness Syndrome,' a book arguing that modern wellness culture has become a tool for shifting blame from systemic failures onto individuals. The authors trace how politicians from Thatcher to Clinton promoted individual responsibility as a shield against governmental accountability. The result is a depoliticized populace that internalizes shame for failing to be sufficiently optimized, rather than questioning inadequate job markets or discriminatory systems. O'Neil connects this to her own work on how employers increasingly control workers' bodies and lives through wellness programs and data collection.
key_points: Wellness culture transforms from self-care into self-blame: if anything is wrong with your life, it's because you failed to optimize yourself
The 'positive mindset' movement pairs perfectly with neoliberal politics, depoliticizing economic precarity by making it a personal failing
The gig economy demands workers appear perpetually happy and employable while taking full responsibility for increasingly unstable conditions
Politicians across the spectrum (Thatcher, Blair, Reagan, Clinton) weaponized individual responsibility to deflect from governmental accountability
O'Neil wonders how online dating and futurist subcultures like Kurzweil's immortality pursuit represent extreme manifestations of this syndrome
money_quotes: It's not just that you are expected to care for yourself, it's that you are blamed if you don't, and conversely, if there's anything at all wrong with your life, then it's because you've failed to sufficiently take care of yourself.

We are asked to enjoy ourselves while we take full personal responsibility for our own wellness, which in the age of the gig economy means we always appear happy to stay lively and infinitely employable under increasingly precarious economic conditions.
God forbid we ever accept any actual limit to our powers of reinvention, because that is equivalent to giving up.
We don't think of ourselves as political victims when we fall prey to a narcissistic worldview in which we are never fit enough, never eating enough organic kale, and never productive enough.
We internalize the shame and the definition of ourselves as 'improperly optimized.'
In the end, we all walk around with tiny little versions of Reagan's welfare queens in our heads.
key_insight: Wellness culture serves as a depoliticizing force that transforms systemic failures into personal inadequacies, making people blame themselves rather than question the systems that constrain them.
